skill.name: github-code-review
skill.description: Coordinates GitHub pull request reviews using GitHub CLI examples and swarm-based review agents.
skill.summary: Run structured, multi-agent GitHub pull request reviews.
skill.author: Claude Code Flow

security_audit.summary: The static scanner produced many false positives because most shell patterns are documentation examples, and the weak-crypto and Windows SAM detections do not match the cited text. However, the skill includes unsafe executable examples, including a webhook handler that passes PR comment text into execSync and GitHub automation that can push fixes or auto-merge PRs. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, but publication should wait for safer examples and stronger warnings.

high_findings.title: Command Injection Risk in Webhook Example
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The webhook handler imports child_process and calls execSync with values taken from GitHub webhook data and PR comments. A user-controlled /swarm comment can be embedded into a shell command, which is dangerous if copied into a real webhook service.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code shows execSync called with event.comment.body through the command variable. The context is an executable JavaScript webhook example that processes external GitHub comment input.

high_findings.title: Automated Repository Mutation Examples
high_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes commands that can commit fixes, push changes, and enable PR auto-merge. These actions are legitimate for review automation but are high impact if run without explicit repository policy controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples explicitly include --commit-fixes, --push-changes, and gh pr merge --auto. The commands are documented workflows, not hidden behavior, but they can modify repository state.

medium_findings.title: Privileged GitHub Token Usage
medium_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The workflow example authenticates GitHub CLI with secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN. This is a normal GitHub Actions pattern, but the token grants repository permissions that can amplify unsafe review automation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The token access is explicit and semantically tied to posting reviews and labels. It is not exfiltrated in the cited text, so the concern is privilege scope rather than theft.

medium_findings.title: Shell-Based Review Workflow Examples
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: Many examples pipe PR data and diffs into npx and gh commands. This is expected for a GitHub automation skill, but users should treat PR content as untrusted input and avoid shell interpolation in production scripts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible documentation examples using GitHub CLI and ruv-swarm. They are mostly legitimate, but they process pull request data through shell variables.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are documentation links for GitHub CLI, RUV Swarm, and Claude Flow. No evidence found that they are used for runtime network calls or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are a documentation list under a Documentation heading. There is no fetch, curl, webhook destination, or credential transfer in this context.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and SAM Detections Are Text False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The cited weak-crypto and Windows SAM findings occur in prose, headings, placeholders, or variable names. No evidence found of cryptographic API use or Windows SAM database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review of the cited lines found documentation text and command examples, not MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or SAM file access. The static labels appear to match substrings rather than executable behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: execSync With Webhook Comment Input
dangerous_patterns.description: The webhook example builds a shell command from event.comment.body. Use argument arrays, strict command parsing, and allowlisted subcommands instead of shell interpolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern combines external webhook data with synchronous shell execution in an example server. This is a direct command injection pattern if used as written.

dangerous_patterns.title: Repository Write Operations From Automation
dangerous_patterns.description: Examples show automated fixes, pushes, labels, reviews, and auto-merge actions. These patterns require least-privilege tokens, branch protection, and human approval gates.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The lines explicitly authenticate with a GitHub token and perform repository-changing operations. The risk depends on deployment permissions and repository policy.

content.user_title: Review GitHub Pull Requests With AI Agents
content.value_statement: Large pull requests are hard to review consistently across security, performance, architecture, and style. This skill provides structured GitHub CLI and swarm-agent workflows for coordinated pull request review.
actual_capabilities: Provides GitHub CLI workflows for collecting pull request metadata and diffs.
Defines review agents for security, performance, architecture, style, and accessibility checks.
Shows how to post pull request comments, reviews, labels, and progress updates.
Includes GitHub Actions examples for automated review workflows.
Documents quality gates, review configuration, and PR comment command patterns.
Suggests templates for structured security findings and review feedback.
limitations: The skill is instruction-only and depends on external tools such as gh, ruv-swarm, and claude-flow.
Several examples require repository write permissions and should not run without policy controls.
The webhook example needs secure command parsing before production use.
It does not replace human review for security-critical or release-blocking decisions.

use_cases.title: Review Large Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Coordinate focused review passes for security, performance, architecture, and maintainability before merging complex changes.

use_cases.title: Add Review Automation to CI
use_cases.description: Use GitHub Actions examples to collect PR context, run review agents, and publish structured feedback.

use_cases.title: Standardize Security Review Feedback
use_cases.description: Use the security review agent and comment templates to make vulnerability feedback clearer and more consistent.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic PR Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single pull request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review pull request 123 using this skill. Focus on changed files, summarize the main risks, and suggest clear next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Run Specialized Review Agents
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request for multi-area review coverage.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review pull request 123 with security, performance, architecture, and style perspectives. Separate findings by severity and include suggested fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare CI Review Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request for GitHub Actions integration.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a GitHub Actions review workflow for pull requests. Include required permissions, safe token handling, and review output posting.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Automation Safety
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request before enabling write operations.
prompt_templates.prompt: Assess this review automation plan before production use. Identify command injection risks, token scope issues, and approval gates for auto-fix or auto-merge.

output_examples.input: Review PR 123 for security and performance problems.
output_examples.output: A severity-ranked review summary with security findings, performance concerns, affected files, and practical remediation steps.

output_examples.input: Set up automated review comments for pull requests.
output_examples.output: A workflow outline that gathers PR context, runs review agents, posts comments, and defines permissions needed for GitHub CLI actions.

output_examples.input: Check whether auto-fix and auto-merge are safe for this repository.
output_examples.output: A risk assessment covering write permissions, branch protection, required approvals, audit logs, and rollback expectations.

best_practices: Use read-only GitHub permissions until review automation has passed controlled testing.
Treat PR titles, bodies, diffs, file names, and comments as untrusted input.
Require human approval before automated fixes, pushes, labels that affect policy, or merges.
anti_patterns: Do not pass PR comments or webhook payload values directly into shell commands.
Do not grant broad repository write permissions to review jobs by default.
Do not let automated review output merge or block PRs without documented quality gates.

faq.question: What tools does this skill expect?
faq.answer: It expects GitHub CLI and external swarm tools such as ruv-swarm and claude-flow.

faq.question: Can it post review comments to GitHub?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples show GitHub CLI and GitHub API commands for comments, reviews, labels, and status updates.

faq.question: Does it execute code by itself?
faq.answer: No. The repository contains a SKILL.md instruction file, but many examples tell users how to run shell commands.

faq.question: Is the webhook example production-ready?
faq.answer: No. The webhook example passes comment text into execSync and needs secure parsing before use.

faq.question: Can it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. It can organize review work, but humans should approve security-sensitive or repository-changing actions.

faq.question: Why is this skill marked high risk?
faq.answer: It includes unsafe command execution examples and repository mutation workflows that need remediation before publication.
